definition: Prefer assembling behavior from smaller components instead of relying on rigid inheritance hierarchies.
ruleOfThumb: When behavior combinations multiply, compose capabilities instead of adding subclasses.

citation.author: Gamma, Helm, Johnson & Vlissides (GoF)
citation.bookTitle: Design Patterns: Elements of Reusable Object-Oriented Software
citation.chapter: Chapter 1: Introduction — Favor Object Composition Over Class Inheritance

questions.prompt: What scaling problem appears in this design as features are combined?

options.text: Behavior is locked into a class hierarchy, so new combinations create awkward overrides and subclass explosions
options.explanation: RubberDuck and RocketDuck need different flight behavior, but inheritance forces them through the same base contract. Swappable behavior components would scale better.

options.text: The quack method should be abstract in Duck so subclasses are required to provide their own implementation
options.explanation: Abstractness does not address the rigid behavior coupling caused by inheritance.

options.text: Bird should be converted to an interface so that unrelated duck types don't inherit unwanted concrete behavior
options.explanation: Using an interface alone does not solve combinatorial subclass growth.

options.text: The problem is that fly should return a boolean to indicate success
options.explanation: Return type is not the main structural challenge shown.

questions.prompt: A game character now needs mix-and-match movement and attack styles. Why does this hierarchy slow feature delivery?

options.text: Each new movement or attack style multiplies subclasses, while composed movement and attack behaviors could be reused directly
options.explanation: Hierarchy encodes behavior combinations as classes, causing combinatorial growth. Composition models movement and attack as interchangeable parts.

options.text: The issue is that these class names are too specific and should be shorter
options.explanation: Naming may improve readability, but it does not fix combination explosion.

options.text: This is fine because subclasses make behavior explicit and avoid runtime wiring complexity
options.explanation: Explicit subclasses help at small scale, but become expensive as combinations and feature variants grow.

options.text: Inheritance is required for performance; composition would always be slower in JavaScript
options.explanation: Performance differences here are negligible compared to maintainability and adaptability concerns.

questions.prompt: Which version gives the team more flexibility to add new abilities without creating new subclasses?

options.text: Version A — adding new movement capabilities keeps forcing special-purpose subclasses
options.explanation: Version A grows by creating deeper types for each variation. Version B swaps behavior objects and keeps the core Vehicle stable.

options.text: Version B — runtime composition hides true type capabilities, so inheritance is safer for extension
options.explanation: Composition intentionally decouples capability selection from class identity, which is what enables safer extension.
